attributes.stem:
A 52-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department after being found collapsed at home for an unknown duration of time. He is known to have a history of alcoholic cardiomyopathy, atrial fibrillation, and alcohol use disorder. His medications include amiodarone, furosemide, and spironolactone.

On examination, he is unconscious and appears critically unwell. His pulse rate is 120/min and irregular, blood pressure 65/40 mmHg, respiratory rate 28/min, SpO2 91% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. He has an elevated jugular venous pressure, bilateral pitting oedema, and bi-basal crepitations. His abdomen is soft and non-tender and is moderately-distended.

He is stabilised and transferred to the Intensive Care Unit. Investigations reveal:

  • Alanine aminotransferase: 1800 U/L (5-40)

  • Aspartate aminotransferase: 2400 U/L (5-40)

  • Alkaline phosphatase: 130 U/L (30-110)

  • Gamma-glutamyl transferase: 95 U/L (5-50)

  • Total bilirubin: 42 µmol/L (3-20)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of his deranged liver function tests?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with markedly elevated aminotransferases in the setting of cardiogenic shock. Ischaemic hepatitis (also known as shock liver or hypoxic hepatitis) is characterised by a dramatic rise in aminotransferases (typically >10 times the upper limit of normal) following hepatic hypoperfusion. The pattern typically shows rapid rise and fall within days once perfusion is restored.

Alcoholic hepatitis typically presents with more modest elevations in aminotransferases (usually <300 U/L), with AST:ALT ratio >2:1. While this patient has significant alcohol use, the dramatic enzyme elevation is more consistent with ischaemic hepatitis.

Drug-induced liver injury can occur secondary to amiodarone, though this typically develops gradually over weeks to months with a mixed hepatocellular-cholestatic pattern leading to more modest enzyme elevation. The markedly raised enzymes and correlation with shock make this diagnosis less likely.

Viral hepatitis typically has a more gradual onset with prodromal symptoms such as fatigue, nausea, and right upper quadrant pain. The rise in aminotransferases is usually less dramatic, and there would likely be a history of risk factors for viral transmission.

Alcohol-associated cirrhosis typically presents with more modest elevations in liver enzymes, usually less than 2-3 times the upper limit of normal. While this patient has a history of alcohol use, the dramatic acute rise in aminotransferases is not typical for cirrhosis, which is a chronic condition with a more indolent course.

The management of ischaemic hepatitis focuses on treating the underlying cause of hypoperfusion, in this case optimising cardiac output and maintaining adequate blood pressure. The liver dysfunction is usually reversible if the underlying cause is addressed promptly.
learningPoints: Ischaemic hepatitis typically presents with a dramatic rise in aminotransferases (>10x upper limit) following a hypotensive episode.
The pattern of liver enzyme derangement in ischaemic hepatitis shows rapid rise and fall within days.
Management of ischaemic hepatitis focuses on treating the underlying cause of hypoperfusion.
references: Waseem N, Chen PH. Hypoxic Hepatitis: A Review and Clinical Update. J Clin Transl Hepatol. 2016 Sep 28;4(3):263-268. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5075010
